What's going on everyone? Bonus video. Six core values. So what I'm going to talk about is why core values are important. Layla goes in a lot more depth about this. I'm just going to give you a brief overview and I'm going to give you a break out of hours. So let's get jiggy. So why should you have them? It tells everyone what you're about. If you're actually about those things. So if you make them and no one knows them, there's no point. I can tell you the top hours off the top of our head, you ask our team what they are. They'll know them off the top of their head because they actually mean something. It's how we live. For us, it's in work and in life. So we live by the same values that we work by. And so I would encourage you that when you're making these values for yourself, these should be the things that you truly live by. These are your core values. And when you're making these, like profit first is not a core value. You know what I mean? It has to be something that's how you make decisions, hard decisions where there's two rights, right? Justice, mercy, variety, consistency. Like those are where the core values are important. So they tell you where you lean in the gray. That's what values do. Okay. And the same thing with hiring and firing. When we looked back at all of our hiring decisions, our firing decisions, we saw that the people who embody these core values are the ones who stuck with us and grew and the ones that we fired. These are the reasons that we fired them because they were massively lacking in one or more of these values. And a lot of times it was a lot of them. When you have large decisions for the business, this is the values that you run by. And these become home base for you, right? And ultimately, at the end of the day, you need to use discernment. This is my disclaimer is use wisdom. Okay. Try and think, what would someone smarter than me do in this situation and then do what that person would do? Okay. So our six core values, grow or die. And like I'm telling you what ours are, you should have your own. And some of these might resonate with you and you can probably say them differently. You know what I mean? Like I could be like, embrace change. Like that would be something that's similar to this, just to communicate it differently. But have your own things and you should believe them, right? Now some of you, I know sometimes you're just like adopted ours because they're like, we believe in your core values. And that's cool because it means like our values are aligned with this community. But grow or die is our first one. And that's because the end of the day, you're growing or you're dying. And so if we're ever stagnating, it means that we are dying and something needs to change. And that goes from a business standpoint, but also from a personal standpoint, like we are either moving forward or moving backwards. And so right now, if your business is not moving forward, it's moving backwards and you need to confront those issues, right? And it's also great to have this as a quartet because when you change things, which is going to happen because business evolves, your team can point to that and you can be like, grow or die. It's on our list. Like it's one of our core values. So either you're in with it and you're willing to change, you're willing to adapt and go with this or you're not and you won't be on this team, right? So back to something moving, please, so I'm going to go to selbst versus sniff and I'm She's just kind of slacking in this area and she's just not performing. I don't know what to say to her and I just don't want to upset her. Say it to her. Hey Lizzie, I don't want to upset you and you mean a lot to us but you're not performing in this cure area and I didn't even want to bring it up but we can't keep going. You just say what you would say to someone else behind their back to their face. It's the easiest hack in the world for how to have hard conversations. Just say what you would normally say, not to them. I have a personal rule that I don't say anything about anyone behind their back that I have not already told them. I used to be someone who would talk negatively about people behind their back and that was something that I'm very disgusted about but it was something that was a habit of mine earlier on. The way I combated that is that I either don't say it or I have to go tell the person. I go tell the people so I feel okay about it. Usually when you confront the person they give their reasons then I understand a little bit better but then I actually provide value to them because I'm like here's where I think this can be improved and here's where I don't agree with you. I can't tell you the amount of times people are like I really respect you for telling me that. So don't sugar coat it with your relationships, with your employees, with your team, with your clients. Just be upfront. Say what you would say behind their back to their face. Be your own boss. This is a lot like taking full responsibility but this is a core value of ours. Are you acting like an owner? Are you taking absolute ownership over all outcomes? Because the moment you don't, the moment you start blaming circumstance and this goes if you have an employee who always blames things, get them out. You're not being your own boss. You're not owning it. That's part of this. If you need me to constantly motivate you, no. Now I'm going to take ownership and always try to motivate everyone but if you need that then you're not going to be a good fit for the team. If I need to micromanage you, if I need to figure out what you're doing all day, it means you're not being enterprising enough, you're not taking enough initiative and you're not acting like a boss. Go the extra mile. Do what everyone else won't. Do that next thing. A lot of times better is better. New is great but new is not common. A lot of times just making a better sandwich gets you to get more people to buy your sandwiches. The thing is most people think their sandwiches are better but they're actually not. They're actually mediocre. Going the extra mile and this comes from service, this goes from how you care for your employees, how you care for your team, how you live your life is like are you the type of person who's going to always have abundant exchange or you're always going to give more that you're asked for. You'll see a commonality between the core habits and our core values. One is more action based on the habits. This is how we make decisions and the values that we measure against. Did this employee really go the extra mile? Was this an example of someone going the extra mile? Probably not. Cool. Let's go. Then when someone does make a hard decision that is true to the core values, you need to applaud them for that. If you make a decision that is hard, you need to say these are our core values and we agreed upon these when you came in. When you're hiring people, you tell them what the core values are so they know what the scoreboard is. This is how we decide. This is how we run things. If you don't agree with this, then this is not going to be a good fit. That's why when they come on, you say I told you these are the values and this is why we made this decision. It's kind of like the constitution for your business. Speed is king. At the end of the day, speed is currency in business. Money loves speed. If it takes you six months to go through something and it takes you something like the people who win, the gym owners who make the most are the ones who are able to go through each module, execute, move to the next one, execute, move to the next one. Rather than letting all this head trash, all their attention take them away from focusing and being effective. You know those people on your team who just get stuff done and that is because of speed and speed is valuable. When you're making decisions, speed is king and you can explain that to an employee. You're just not fast enough. You're not embodying this. You're not going the extra mile. You're not being your own boss. You can see how these values help you make decisions, explain decisions, explain hiring, explain firing, and make the hard decisions in business. Then half humility. This is the last one that we added and I think it's incredibly important because when we looked at, because this was a more nuanced one that came in, because we had some people as we've grown that were individual contributors. They were stars, but they didn't work well with the team because they thought they were always right, which made them uncoachable because you have to admit that you have deficits in order to grow. There has to be a gap in order for you to get better. If you have no humility and you're all pride, then the day you come in, that skill set is the max you'll ever have because you never admit that you're not good enough. We're all not good enough. We all fall short. You have to be able to admit that. You have to have a team who's able to admit that. All of these values start with you. If you are prideful, having a value of humility, it's not going to work. No one's going to believe you. You're not walking the talk. If you're slow to get back to everything, speed is king. It's not going to be like no one's going to do it. If you don't go the extra mile for your team, they're not going to go your extra mile for the employees, for your customers. If you don't act like an owner and show up ready to win, ready to do more than everyone else's every day, they aren't either. If you start gossiping and having back channels and whispers and you're always passive aggressive and you don't say what you mean and there's two lines of dialogue. What you're saying and what you mean, you can't have being honest and being transparent and not sugarcoating it as a core value because no one's going to live it. If you can't change because you're so rigid, this is going to be one that's usually not the entrepreneur's issue, then that can't be a core value. Those are our six core values and that's a brief overview that I hope explained that to you in a very small nutshell. That is what we believe. Layla and I are together and we will say these values to each other. She'll just be like, babe, don't sugarcoat it. Just tell me like it is. Babe, thanks for only the extra mile. You know what I mean? Having that. Or sometimes she'll call me, I'll be like, babe, have humility. I'll be like, all right, all right, all right. This is for work and life and when you make these values, it should be about who you are and everyone should know that. When you communicate that to your tribe, they will believe in you because of what you stand for and not who you are because it becomes something greater than you and that's what you want.
